displayTitle: 2026-03-11 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Infrastructure Funding and Financing Amendment Bill
topic: Infrastructure Funding and Financing Amendment Bill
description: Wednesday 11 March 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee Infrastructure Funding and Financing Amendment Bill Chris Chamberlain Cameron Partners Limited on behalf of the Ratepayer Assistance Scheme Steering Group Hugo Ellis, Partner Cameron Partners Scott Necklen, Interim CE LGNZ Mark Butcher, CE LGFA New Zealanders for a Democratic Economy Dr. Nathan McCluskey, Co-chair Transitnext Limited Brian Ferguson, Director Steve Kelly, Equiland Holdings Pty Ltd Richard Mead John McDonald-Wharry
descriptionSummary: Infrastructure Funding and Financing Amendment Bill. Chris Chamberlain. Cameron Partners Limited on behalf of the Ratepayer Assistance Scheme Steering Group

Quite a, um, we've Got something to Circulate. Challenging process actually That's there. It does not argue a for or against the bill. What it tries to do is identify with changes or in fact, the original 2020 Act would produce effects that undermine the mechanisms long-term value to New Zealanders effects that might not be visible until a project is already underway or until the first community dispute lands in court.

It considers impacts on all of the main stakeholders, the developers, councils, levy, payers, investors, water organizations, Maori landowners. My intent was to be as comprehensive, uh, and not be advocating for any one group. I would like to talk to the document structure just to help people, um, understand it.

I'm happy to talk about that in more detail later. Um, and there are two key findings tables on pages five and seven, which I think are a useful quick reference.

They map every substantive change that the bill makes side by side with the 2020 Act across all the stakeholder groups. On pages eight and nine, I've highlighted something I've called the stacking problem, where multiple special purpose vehicles can independently levy the same properties with no cumulative oversight.

I think this particular issue is very likely to damage the bill's credibility and durability if it isn't addressed. The second, the, the third section really is pages 11 through 18. These are the proposals. Um, they're one or two pages each. They're quite succinct.

They described the problem that I was able to identify and provide some recommendations for fixing that. But each one is aimed at staying within the bill's intent rather than trying to undermine or reverse it. Um, I mean, there are good sides to this bill and there are also risks.

And I said, I think that is what I'm trying to highlight with this work. For an overall summary, please see the conclusion on page 21. Um, there are a number of, of proposals there. Um, I would like to highlight what I think are the most important.

Firstly, the, the cumulative levy cap, which is the first of them on page 11. This specifically addresses the stacking risk and the durability, therefore, of this whole mechanism. Um, the next one that I think is pretty important is structured notifications.

Um, the problem, one of the problems with this legislation as it takes away all of the checks and balances that were in the original 2020 Act. So this structured notification process provides a time bound process with a very tight timeframe, which gives, and yet it also gives people the opportunity to contribute and understand what's going on.

The levy commissioner, the Levy Register, these are just necessary things for running a mechanism like this and very important. And finally, the public and your reports from SPVs, providing transparency and accountability. Those would be my top four. Okay. I snuck one and five of the proposals I have there.

Um, so the RAs, uh, can materially enhance the efficiency and applicability of IFF projects. Um, the RAs has been developed to address a number of council issues. So things like race postponement, um, deferred development contributions and property improvement loans that have a public benefit, for example, solar power. Uh, and there's other number of other, uh, categories that we could talk to as well.

And in principle it can also be applied to IFF projects. Um, there is fundamentally the same as an IFF responsible SPV. Um, it raises finance from the debt markets and is off balance sheet. Uh, it uses the finance to keep councils whole by covering their charges and supporting them to make capital investments.

Um, it also levies rate payers to get repaid as well. Um, equally in much the same way as the local government funding agency finances councils. Um, the RES could and principle be centralized finance vehicle for the IFF responsible, um, SPVs, uh, where the RAs can add value is that it is an established or what would be an established national organization.

So effectively a shared service for the sector, um, with the structure systems, processes and personnel, uh, in place to raise debt, make payments and invoice rate payers efficiently and effectively. Um, it will have the statutory powers, uh, enabling it to charge a levy that is persu, uh, with local government rate charges.

Um, very much the same as an IFF responsible SPV because of its structure, purpose living powers and councils joined in several guarantee it will have a very high credit rating. Um, and it's able to obtain very low cost financing and pass that benefit on to rate payers because it will issue homogenous securities.

It will be able to develop a wide and deep debt investor base, um, enhancing margins and security of access to debt markets. The RAs will be a low cost operation and achieve scale across all of its products, maximizing revenue and cost synergies. Um, its specialist IT systems and obviously the relationships that councils have, uh, with rate payers will enable it to charge a levy to individual property owners across New Zealand.

Avoiding administrative issues for local authorities, which may not have the systems in place to easily leave levy specific rate payers. In summary, lower establishment costs, lower financing rates, and existing invoicing systems of the RAs would significantly enhance the applicability IFF to smaller projects and more local authorities and specific subsets of local authority rate payer basis.

In addition, in the greenhouse housing context, the RES and the divert dcs under the rate payer assistance schemes are likely to be able to, um, likely to be a simpler lower cost mechanism for developers to access, um, development, contribution financing. Okay. Can you hear me now? Yes, we can. Dr. McCloskey, thank you. Thank you for your patience. Um, you've got, uh, 10 minutes. Go for it. Great. Thank you. Uh, grateful for this opportunity to, to speak to, um, the sleep committee today. And, uh, apologies for the technical difficulties earlier.

Look there. Um, I guess the purpose of the, um, the, um, the purpose of this piece of, um, legislation, the IFF, the, um, infrastructure funding the finance bill, really is to ensure, I guess superficially it's to ensure that the funding of water infrastructure in New Zealand, uh, can take place, uh, now and into the future.

Uh, but really, uh, when I look at the bill, uh, we've concluded that, that its objective really is to shift a, a public resource into private hands. And this bill makes that possible. So effectively, what can happen now is that, um, in independent companies will be able to, uh, provide the infrastructure that the government has been responsible for.

Because let's be clear, water is a fundamental human right. The access to clean drinking water is a responsibility that government has, uh, to its constituents. And what this will do is it will enable, uh, a lot more of what we're seeing in Wellington with the Veolia situation.

And, uh, the, um, I guess the debacle with water and it's causing to Wellington's coast. Uh, we, we believe that government should be providing that this piece of, um, legislation is effectively, uh, by creating SPVs. And it doesn't specify whether the public or private, so they, they could easily be private, uh, then with the profit that could be gained from providing water provision of, of water accrues to indi individual private companies.

But if the infrastructure, uh, falls over as we're seeing spectacularly in Wellington, in the liability or the levies, uh, can be then put on future water provision. In other words, the public then, then becomes responsible for, for paying for that. Now, we know, uh, from, for example, in the uk, PFIs the, um, private financing initiatives make it clear that it's two to 5% more expensive to provide these things through private sector than public borrowing.

And so, uh, we are really concerned about that shift of responsibility away from the public to the private sector who, who ultimately aren't accountable because they're not accountable to the people, only to their shareholders. And, and they can pull out of agreements, um, in a way that public sector can't.

And that's why it's safer, we believe, with the public sector. And we think that, that, um, you know, what we've seen in the uk, for example, is a good example is, is by, is, is costs that are as much as double what they would have been had the borrowing been through the public sector. And there's plenty of research on that that we can provide if you are interested.

Uh, so we think that, and you're probably looking for alternative ways to pay for this, because these are big bills. Um, and there was some legislation in place, impact assessments were done around that, um, in relation to three waters, for example. And, and local water done well is the replacement for that.

But we feel that this is, is not a good replacement for that because of the shift of liability to levies, which can be levied on that future water against the public. And even in some cases, um, Maori land, for example. Now, what we are concerned about is, is that the, the liability then leaves, uh, the public sector with, with, uh, public accountability and, and resides then in the private sector, but that ultimately it will come back to the public sector if that falls over.

And, and so we, uh, we are speaking against this bill. We're in a, we're in opposition to it. We think there are other ways to, to help fund this infrastructure. For example, there are lots of contributors to the water system, not just drinking water, but say storm water, for example. The roads contribute a lot to that.

Um, yeah, all, all of it. And so, but, but road user charges are not levied, uh, against the water, the stormwater that's created as a result of their construction. And we'd like to see that to happen, uh, happen as a, as, uh, an alternative, for example. But, uh, bottom line is that, you know, history offers kind of cautionary examples of what happens when, when water is privatized, which this bill was really designed to do through the SPV system.

Uh, and the impact assessment we think was done in 2019 is pretty accurate. And we would like those, uh, concerns to be taken on board. Um, and, and that's, that's really, um, the thing that we wanted to focus on. Most of all of the aspects we want to focus on.

You've read the submission, uh, we're happy to answer questions. Okay. Thank, thank you. I'm gonna go to Dr. Deborah Russell. Uh, just a, a straightforward one. So New Zealanders for a democratic economy was established in order to, uh, create public awareness around, uh, an alternative to the capital finance system.

Uh, because, uh, we believe that the, the market is, uh, a good servant, but a very poor master. And what we are seeing is that, uh, New Zealand government, uh, local, regional, and, and national, uh, moving away from responsibility to provide fundamental, uh, resources which are necessary, they're fundamental human rights like water, and, and shifting the responsibility of that to the private sector.

When the private sector will, as we've seen in Wellington with Veolia, for example, is asked to accept responsibility for the failures in that, then they, they're not held accountable and they can't be held accountable by the public. They're accountable, really only shareholders. And, and so what, what New Zealanders for Democratic economy is saying is that we want to see, uh, our economy be publicly accountable.

Our economy should be answerable to the people. Um, not private companies. Private companies aren't, uh, elected by government. Governments are elected by the public. And so the public should be, should have the risk, should have oversight, democratic oversight, and hold them accountable.
